---
title: "Packages: NumPy, pandas, and matplotlib"
subtitle: "Machine Learning in Finance · Session 3"
# Not self-contained: quarto-live's Pyodide runtime cannot resolve its worker
# from a data: URI, so embed-resources is off. Ship session_03.html together
# with session_03_files/ and data/, and SERVE OVER HTTP (double-click
# serve.cmd, or publish to a URL). The live code cells do not run from file://.
#
# This deck loads numpy, pandas and matplotlib into the browser runtime, which
# is a larger download than Sessions 1 and 2 (roughly 20 MB, once per page
# load, about 10 seconds on a good connection). Open the deck and run one cell
# a few minutes before class so it is warm.
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme/mlfin.scss]
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    center: false
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    background-transition: none
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: false
    progress: true
    hash: true
    history: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    auto-stretch: false
engine: jupyter
pyodide:
  packages:
    - numpy
    - pandas
    - matplotlib
  resources:
    - data/prices.csv
    - data/aapl_2024_closes.csv
  cell-options:
    autorun: true
    startover: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Last session, you wrote this

```{python}
#| include: false
# House style for every precomputed figure in this deck.
import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

MLFIN_STYLE = {
    "figure.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "axes.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "savefig.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "font.family": ["Segoe UI", "Arial", "sans-serif"],
    "font.size": 12, "text.color": "#141413", "axes.edgecolor": "#c3c2b7",
    "axes.labelcolor": "#52514e", "axes.titlecolor": "#141413",
    "axes.titlesize": 13, "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True, "grid.color": "#e6e5df", "grid.linewidth": 0.8,
    "axes.axisbelow": True,
    "xtick.color": "#898781", "ytick.color": "#898781",
    "xtick.labelsize": 10.5, "ytick.labelsize": 10.5,
    "legend.frameon": False, "legend.fontsize": 10.5,
}
plt.rcParams.update(MLFIN_STYLE)

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

_data = Path("../data/prices.csv")
if not _data.exists():
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Missing ../data/prices.csv - clone the full course repository, or "
        "run tools/build_dataset.py from the repository root."
    )
PX = pd.read_csv(_data, parse_dates=["date"])
WIDE = PX.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close")
RET = WIDE.pct_change().dropna()

BLUE = "#1c5cab"
BLUE_LIGHT = "#2a78d6"
AMBER = "#b8860b"
GREY = "#c3c2b7"
INK = "#141413"
```

```{pyodide}
#| include: false
# House style for the live figures, so they match the printed ones.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "axes.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "font.family": ["Segoe UI", "Arial", "sans-serif"], "font.size": 12,
    "text.color": "#141413", "axes.edgecolor": "#c3c2b7",
    "axes.labelcolor": "#52514e", "axes.titlecolor": "#141413",
    "axes.titlesize": 13, "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True, "grid.color": "#e6e5df", "grid.linewidth": 0.8,
    "axes.axisbelow": True,
    "xtick.color": "#898781", "ytick.color": "#898781",
    "xtick.labelsize": 10.5, "ytick.labelsize": 10.5,
    "legend.frameon": False, "legend.fontsize": 10.5,
    "figure.dpi": 100,
})
```

Volatility for one stock, built by hand out of loops:

```{pyodide}
def volatility(prices):
    returns = []
    for i in range(1, len(prices)):
        returns.append((prices[i] - prices[i - 1]) / prices[i - 1])
    mean = sum(returns) / len(returns)
    squared = 0
    for r in returns:
        squared += (r - mean) ** 2
    return (squared / len(returns)) ** 0.5

volatility([183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48])
```


# Now do the whole risk report

The real data is one file: **eleven stocks, ten years, 27,676 rows.**

```text
date,ticker,close,volume
2015-01-02,AAPL,24.1926,212818400
2015-01-05,AAPL,23.5111,257142000
...
2024-12-31,XOM,102.3799,11302800
```

With lists and loops you would first have to split every line at the commas,
turn the text into numbers, and sort the rows into eleven separate lists. All
before computing anything.


# The same answer, in three lines {.smaller}

```python
prices = pd.read_csv("data/prices.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
daily = prices.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close").pct_change()
daily.std().sort_values(ascending=False)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
_p = pd.read_csv("../data/prices.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
_d = _p.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close").pct_change()
print(_d.std().sort_values(ascending=False).round(4).to_string())
```

::: {.muted .small}
None of that is meant to make sense yet. It is where we are going.
:::


# That is what a package is

A **package** is code somebody else wrote, that you can use by name. Three of
them do most of the work in data analysis:

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**NumPy** <span>numbers, vectors and matrices, and the maths on them</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**pandas** <span>tables with named columns, like a spreadsheet you can program</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**matplotlib** <span>figures, so you can see what you found</span>
:::
:::

::: {.muted .small}
There are thousands of others, and you will meet more of them as the course goes
on, including **scikit-learn**, the one that fits models.
:::


# And they live on your machine

Packages are not part of Python. Somebody has to install them, and in Colab
somebody already did.

::: {.keypoint}
At the exam you sit with **your own laptop, VS Code, and no internet**. So today
starts there: your machine, your packages, and how to look things up when you
cannot search for them.
:::


# Today

By the end of this session you can:

- work in **VS Code** on your own machine, and find documentation offline
- use **NumPy** for arrays, and for the matrix algebra behind every model
- use **pandas** to load, filter and summarise a real table of prices
- use **matplotlib** to turn a result into a figure somebody else can read

<br>

And the case reaches all eleven stocks at once.


# 1 · Your own machine {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 1
:::

::: {.divider-note}
VS Code, packages, and finding things out without the internet.
:::


# Why leave Colab

Colab still works, and the homework stays there. But:

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**The exam** <span>three hours, your laptop, VS Code, offline. It should not be the first time.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**The editor helps you** <span>it completes names and shows documentation as you type</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**It is yours** <span>your files, your packages, nothing to upload, no session that expires</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**No internet needed** <span>once it is installed, it works on a train</span>
:::
:::


# Three things, not one

The commonest confusion is thinking VS Code *is* Python. It is not:

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1000" height="200" viewBox="0 0 1000 200" role="img" aria-label="Python, VS Code, and the Python extension are three separate installs that work together">
  <rect x="30" y="40" width="250" height="110" rx="10" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="155" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="600" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">Python</text>
  <text x="155" y="107" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">the language itself,</text>
  <text x="155" y="127" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">the thing that runs code</text>
  <rect x="375" y="40" width="250" height="110" rx="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="500" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="600" fill="#141413" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">VS Code</text>
  <text x="500" y="107" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">the editor: where you</text>
  <text x="500" y="127" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">type and read the code</text>
  <rect x="720" y="40" width="250" height="110" rx="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="845" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="600" fill="#141413" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">Python extension</text>
  <text x="845" y="107" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">teaches the editor about</text>
  <text x="845" y="127" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">Python: run, hints, docs</text>
  <line x1="285" y1="95" x2="365" y2="95" stroke="#898781" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <line x1="630" y1="95" x2="710" y2="95" stroke="#898781" stroke-width="1.6"/>
</svg>
:::

The setup guide installs all three.


# You still work in notebooks

VS Code opens `.ipynb` files: the same cells, the same Run buttons, the same
output underneath as in Colab.

::: {.keypoint}
Nothing about how you write code changes. Only where it runs. The exam is
written in a notebook too.
:::

::: {.muted .small}
The first time you open one, VS Code asks which Python to use. Pick the one you
installed, and it remembers.
:::


# Installing a package

Packages come from a public archive called PyPI. You install one **once**, and
then it is on your machine for good:

```python
%pip install pandas
```

::: {.muted .small}
Run that in a notebook cell, like any other code. The `%` marks it as an
instruction to the notebook rather than to Python. Your setup guide already
installed the three we need, so you should not have to type it at all today.
:::

::: {.keypoint}
Installing and importing are different. You **install** a package once, ever.
You **import** it in every notebook that uses it.
:::


# The import line

`import` brings a package in. `as` gives it a short name:

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.mean([1, 2, 3, 4])
```

::: {.muted .small}
`np`, `pd` and `plt` are conventions, not rules. Everybody uses them and every
tutorial assumes them, so use them too.
:::


# When it cannot find it

```python
import pandas as pd
```
```text
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'
```

Python is telling you the truth: it looked, and it is not there. Install it, then
run the import again.

::: {.muted .small}
Same habit as Session 1. The message names the thing that is missing.
:::


# Your data sits next to your notebook

```text
mlfin/
├── analysis.ipynb
└── data/
    └── prices.csv
```

```python
prices = pd.read_csv("data/prices.csv")
```

::: {.muted .small}
No upload step any more. If Python says `FileNotFoundError`, the file is
somewhere other than where you said. Open the **folder** in VS Code, not just
the file, and the paths work.
:::


# Looking things up

pandas has hundreds of functions, each with its own arguments. Nobody memorises
them, and professionals look things up all day. At the exam you have no
internet.

::: {.keypoint}
Two things cover you. Before the exam I will give you a **cheatsheet** with
every function you are likely to need. And the documentation for each package is
already **inside the package**, on your machine.
:::

::: {.muted .small}
The cheatsheet is on the course page, under
[Cheatsheet](../cheatsheet.html){target="_blank"}: every function we have used,
with a worked example of each.
:::


# VS Code shows it as you type

Rest the mouse on any name:

<div class="nb-mock nb-wide">
<div class="nb-cell nb-code"><div class="nb-gutter">1</div><div class="nb-body">daily.std().sort_values(<span style="background:#fdf3d7">ascending</span>=False)</div></div>
</div>

<div class="hovercard">
<div class="hc-sig">sort_values(<span class="hc-arg">ascending</span>: bool = True, ...) &rarr; Series</div>
<div class="hc-body">Sort by the values.<br><br><span class="hc-p">ascending</span> : bool, default True<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;If True, sort values in ascending order, otherwise descending.</div>
</div>

That card is not from the internet. It is the text that ships inside pandas, the
same text `help()` prints.


# The official documentation

For learning something new, rather than recalling a detail, the websites are
much better. They have a written guide, worked examples, and pictures:

- `docs.python.org` for the language itself
- `numpy.org/doc` for arrays and linear algebra
- `pandas.pydata.org/docs` for tables
- `matplotlib.org` for figures, including a **gallery** of example charts

::: {.muted .small}
Use these at home while you work. They are also downloadable as a single file,
if you want them offline. All of them, and more, are linked from
[Resources](../resources.html){target="_blank"} on the course page.
:::


# 2 · NumPy {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 2
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Arrays: doing arithmetic to a whole list at once.
:::


# Somebody already wrote your function

Your `volatility` is a standard deviation, and standard deviations have been
written many times before:

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

returns = np.array([0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006])
np.std(returns)
```

::: {.muted .small}
Writing it yourself was how you learned what it means. From now on you use the
version that is already written, tested and fast.
:::


# A list, and an array

An **array** is NumPy's version of a list: same idea, all numbers, and the whole
thing moves at once.

```{pyodide}
close = np.array([183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48])
close
```

You build it from a list, and you index and slice it exactly like a list.
Everything from Session 1 still works.


# Arithmetic on the whole array

Here is the difference. Multiply an array, and every element is multiplied:

```{pyodide}
close = np.array([183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48])
close * 2
```

No loop, no empty list, no `.append`. In Session 2 this was three lines.


# Two arrays at once

```{pyodide}
a = np.array([100.0, 200.0, 300.0])
b = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
a - b
```

Position by position: `100-1`, `200-2`, `300-3`.


# Every daily return, in one line

Slicing from Session 1, plus that arithmetic:

```{pyodide}
close = np.array([183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48])
returns = (close[1:] - close[:-1]) / close[:-1]
returns
```

`close[1:]` is every day from the second. `close[:-1]` is every day except the
last. Subtracting them lines each day up against the day before.


# The same idea, drawn

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1000" height="225" viewBox="0 0 1000 225" role="img" aria-label="close[1:] sits one place to the right of close[:-1], so each day is paired diagonally with the day before it">
  <text x="20" y="45" font-size="15" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">close[1:]</text>
  <text x="20" y="120" font-size="15" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">close[:-1]</text>
  <text x="20" y="196" font-size="15" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" font-weight="600">difference</text>
  <g stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.3" stroke-dasharray="4 4" fill="none">
    <path d="M 215 93 L 360 62"/>
    <path d="M 375 93 L 520 62"/>
    <path d="M 535 93 L 680 62"/>
    <path d="M 695 93 L 840 62"/>
  </g>
  <g fill="#b8860b">
    <polygon points="366,60 353,57 356,68"/>
    <polygon points="526,60 513,57 516,68"/>
    <polygon points="686,60 673,57 676,68"/>
    <polygon points="846,60 833,57 836,68"/>
  </g>
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace" font-size="16">
    <rect x="300" y="20" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <rect x="460" y="20" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <rect x="620" y="20" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <rect x="780" y="20" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <text x="375" y="46" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0d366b">182.19</text>
    <text x="535" y="46" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0d366b">179.87</text>
    <text x="695" y="46" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0d366b">179.15</text>
    <text x="855" y="46" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0d366b">183.48</text>
    <rect x="140" y="95" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <rect x="300" y="95" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <rect x="460" y="95" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <rect x="620" y="95" width="150" height="38" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.4"/>
    <text x="215" y="121" text-anchor="middle" fill="#141413">183.56</text>
    <text x="375" y="121" text-anchor="middle" fill="#141413">182.19</text>
    <text x="535" y="121" text-anchor="middle" fill="#141413">179.87</text>
    <text x="695" y="121" text-anchor="middle" fill="#141413">179.15</text>
    <text x="375" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" fill="#52514e">-1.37</text>
    <text x="535" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" fill="#52514e">-2.32</text>
    <text x="695" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" fill="#52514e">-0.72</text>
    <text x="855" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" fill="#52514e">+4.33</text>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.1" stroke-dasharray="3 4">
    <line x1="855" y1="62" x2="855" y2="180"/>
  </g>
</svg>
:::

The arrows show the pairing: each day is lined up against the day before
it, which is exactly what the subtraction does.


# Summarising an array

The things you built by hand in Session 2 are already there:

```{pyodide}
returns = np.array([0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006])
print("mean :", returns.mean())
print("std  :", returns.std())
print("min  :", returns.min())
print("max  :", returns.max())
```

::: {.muted .small}
`returns.std()` is your whole `volatility` function.
:::


# A question asked of every element

Compare an array to a number, and you get an answer for each element:

```{pyodide}
returns = np.array([0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006])
returns > 0
```

This is the Session 2 `if`, applied to everything at once instead of one item at
a time inside a loop.


# Counting with that answer

```{pyodide}
returns = np.array([0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006])
(returns > 0).sum()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Adding up `True` and `False` counts the `True`s, because `True` counts as one.
That is the up-day counter from Session 2, in one line.
:::


# Annualising, while we are here

Daily volatility is a small number. Finance quotes it per year, by multiplying
by the square root of the number of trading days:

```{pyodide}
daily_vol = 0.0143
daily_vol * np.sqrt(252)
```

::: {.muted .small}
So Apple's 1.4% a day is about 23% a year. You will see `np.sqrt(252)` a lot.
:::


# Your turn [1]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Compute the daily returns from these prices.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
close = np.array([100.0, 102.0, 101.0, 104.0, 103.5])

returns = ...
returns
```


# Your turn 1: answer

```python
close = np.array([100.0, 102.0, 101.0, 104.0, 103.5])

returns = (close[1:] - close[:-1]) / close[:-1]
returns
```

**Four returns from five prices:** `+2.0%`, `-1.0%`, `+3.0%`, `-0.5%`.


# From lists of numbers to tables of them

So far, one row of numbers. NumPy's other subject is the **matrix**: rows and
columns of them.

::: {.keypoint}
You have done all of this maths in econometrics. Nothing on the next few slides
is a new idea. What is new is that each one takes a single line.
:::


# The prerequisites {.smaller}

::: {.recap-grid}
::: {.recap-item}
**Vector**
A row or column of numbers. One stock's returns, or a set of portfolio weights.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Matrix**
A rectangle of numbers, `n` rows by `p` columns. One row per observation.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Transpose**
Flip rows and columns. An `n × p` becomes a `p × n`.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Dot product**
Multiply pairwise, then add up. Weights times returns is a portfolio return.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Matrix product**
Only fits if the first one's columns match the second one's rows.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Inverse**
The matrix that undoes another one. Exists only if no column is redundant.
:::
:::


# A matrix is a list of rows

```{pyodide}
X = np.array([[1.0, 0.012],
              [1.0, -0.004],
              [1.0, 0.008]])
print(X)
print("shape:", X.shape)
```

Three rows, two columns. `shape` always tells you which is which.


# Reaching into it

```{pyodide}
print("one element  :", X[0, 1])
print("first row    :", X[0, :])
print("second column:", X[:, 1])
```

::: {.muted .small}
`[row, column]`, and `:` means "all of them". The same slicing as a list, once
per direction.
:::


# Transpose

`.T` flips it:

```{pyodide}
print("X   is", X.shape)
print("X.T is", X.T.shape)
X.T
```

A 3 by 2 becomes a 2 by 3. You use this constantly, because it is how you make
two shapes fit together.


# Multiplying: the dot product

Multiply pairwise and add up. In finance that is a **portfolio return**: weights
times returns.

```{pyodide}
weights = np.array([0.5, 0.3, 0.2])
returns = np.array([0.012, -0.004, 0.008])

weights @ returns
```

::: {.muted .small}
`0.5 × 0.012 + 0.3 × (-0.004) + 0.2 × 0.008`. The `@` symbol is matrix
multiplication, and for two vectors that is the dot product.
:::


# The same `@`, on a matrix

```{pyodide}
X = np.array([[1.0, 0.012],
              [1.0, -0.004],
              [1.0, 0.008]])
b = np.array([0.001, 1.2])

X @ b
```

::: {.muted .small}
Three rows in, three numbers out: one per row. That is a regression's fitted
values, for the whole sample in one go.
:::


# Shapes have to fit

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="920" height="235" viewBox="0 0 920 235" role="img" aria-label="An n by p matrix times a p by one vector gives an n by one vector; the inner dimensions must match">
  <text x="155" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13.5" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">n observations, p columns</text>
  <text x="370" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13.5" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">one number per column</text>
  <text x="535" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13.5" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">one number per row</text>
  <rect x="60" y="42" width="190" height="105" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="155" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">X</text>
  <text x="155" y="175" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">n × <tspan fill="#7a5a08" font-weight="600">p</tspan></text>
  <text x="290" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="26" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">×</text>
  <rect x="330" y="42" width="80" height="105" rx="6" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="370" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" fill="#141413" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">b</text>
  <text x="370" y="175" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif"><tspan fill="#7a5a08" font-weight="600">p</tspan> × 1</text>
  <text x="452" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="26" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">=</text>
  <rect x="495" y="42" width="80" height="105" rx="6" fill="#fbf4e4" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="535" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" fill="#7a5a08" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">y</text>
  <text x="535" y="175" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">n × 1</text>
  <path d="M 168 186 C 175 205, 350 205, 358 186" fill="none" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="263" y="225" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#7a5a08" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">these two must match</text>
</svg>
:::

Nearly every NumPy error you will ever see is this rule.


# The inverse

`np.linalg.inv` gives the matrix that undoes another one:

```{pyodide}
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0],
              [1.0, 3.0]])

np.linalg.inv(A) @ A
```

::: {.muted .small}
The result is the **identity**: ones down the diagonal, zeros elsewhere. The
off-diagonal one prints as something like `-1.11e-16`, which is scientific
notation for a decimal point followed by fifteen zeros and a 111. Computers
store decimals approximately, so a value that should be exactly zero often comes
out this tiny instead. Read it as zero.
:::


# When there is no inverse

```python
B = np.array([[1.0, 2.0],
              [2.0, 4.0]])

np.linalg.inv(B)
```
```text
LinAlgError: Singular matrix
```

The second column is just twice the first, so it adds nothing.

::: {.muted .small}
You have met this before under another name: perfect multicollinearity, the
regression that will not run.
:::


# OLS, in one line

Your econometrics course wrote the estimator like this:

$$ \hat{\beta} = \left(X'X\right)^{-1} X'y $$

```python
beta = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)
```

Read it against the formula: `X.T` is `X'`, `@` is multiplication, and `solve`
does the inverting and multiplying in one safer step.


# A real regression: market beta

Apple's daily return against the market's. The slope is the **beta** you know
from the CAPM:

```{pyodide}
px = pd.read_csv("data/prices.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
rets = px.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close").pct_change().dropna()

y = rets["AAPL"].values
x = rets["SPY"].values
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(x)), x])

alpha, beta = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)
print("alpha:", round(alpha, 6), " beta:", round(beta, 3))
```

::: {.muted .small}
Change the ticker and run it again: Nvidia gives 1.75, JPMorgan 1.12, Coca-Cola
0.60. Above one moves more than the market, below one moves less.
:::


# Why arrays are worth it {.smaller}

Squaring a million numbers, first with a loop and then with an array:

```{python}
#| echo: true
import time
xs, arr = list(range(1_000_000)), np.arange(1_000_000)

t0 = time.perf_counter()
total = 0
for v in xs:
    total += v * v
loop = time.perf_counter() - t0

t0 = time.perf_counter()
total = (arr * arr).sum()
vec = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"loop: {loop:.3f} s     numpy: {vec:.4f} s     {loop / vec:.0f} times faster")
```

::: {.muted .small}
NumPy hands the whole array to compiled code, instead of stepping through it one
Python statement at a time.
:::


# 3 · pandas {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 3
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Tables with names on the columns.
:::


# What pandas adds

NumPy gives you numbers in a grid. pandas puts **labels** on that grid:

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Series** <span>one column of values, each with a label. A list and a dictionary at once.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**DataFrame** <span>several Series side by side, sharing one index. A spreadsheet you can program.</span>
:::
:::


# A Series

```{pyodide}
import pandas as pd

vol = pd.Series({"AAPL": 0.0143, "KO": 0.0081, "NVDA": 0.0331})
vol
```

::: {.muted .small}
Built straight from a Session 2 dictionary.
:::


# A Series does both jobs

```{pyodide}
print(vol["NVDA"])       # by name, like a dictionary
print(vol.mean())        # summarises itself, like an array
vol * np.sqrt(252)       # and does maths all at once
```

That combination is the whole reason pandas exists.


# A DataFrame

```{pyodide}
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "ticker": ["AAPL", "KO", "NVDA"],
    "vol": [0.0143, 0.0081, 0.0331],
    "beta": [1.21, 0.60, 1.75],
})
df
```

Columns have names, and different columns can hold different types.


# Reading a real file

In Sessions 1 and 2 the data arrived in a cell somebody wrote for you. Not any
more:

```{pyodide}
prices = pd.read_csv("data/prices.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
prices.head()
```

::: {.muted .small}
`.head()` shows the first five rows. `parse_dates` turns that column from text
into real dates, which is what lets you sort and slice by time later.
:::


# Look before you compute

```{pyodide}
print("shape  :", prices.shape)
print()
print(prices.dtypes)
```

::: {.muted .small}
27,676 rows and 4 columns, and `dtypes` says what each column holds. A price
column that arrived as text is a common and silent bug.
:::


# One column

```{pyodide}
prices["close"].head()
```

A name in square brackets gives back a **Series**: that column, with its row
labels kept.


# Several columns

A **list** of names inside the brackets gives a smaller **DataFrame**:

```{pyodide}
prices[["date", "close"]].head()
```

::: {.muted .small}
One set of brackets for the indexing, one for the list. That is why there are
two of them.
:::


# Keeping only some rows

A condition gives `True` or `False` for every row, just as in NumPy. Put it in
brackets to keep the `True` ones:

```{pyodide}
aapl = prices[prices["ticker"] == "AAPL"]
aapl.head()
```

::: {.muted .small}
27,676 rows tested at once.
:::


# Adding a column

Assign to a name that does not exist yet, and it appears:

```{pyodide}
aapl = prices[prices["ticker"] == "AAPL"].copy()
aapl["ret"] = aapl["close"].pct_change()
aapl[["date", "close", "ret"]].head()
```

::: {.muted .small}
`.pct_change()` is the daily return: the shift-and-divide you have now written
by hand twice.
:::


# Why the `.copy()`

Leave it out, and pandas complains:

```python
aapl = prices[prices["ticker"] == "AAPL"]     # no .copy()
aapl["ret"] = aapl["close"].pct_change()
```
```text
SettingWithCopyWarning: A value is trying to be set on a copy of a
slice from a DataFrame.
```

`aapl` might be a window onto `prices` rather than a table of its own, so pandas
cannot tell whether you also meant to change the original. `.copy()` says "give
me my own table", and the warning goes away.


# The first one is missing

The first day has no day before it, so its return is `NaN`, "not a number":

```{pyodide}
aapl["ret"].head(3)
```

::: {.muted .small}
`NaN` means "no value here". It is not zero and it is not an error, and most
summary methods skip it for you.
:::


# Apple's volatility

```{pyodide}
print("daily     :", aapl["ret"].std().round(5))
print("annualised:", (aapl["ret"].std() * np.sqrt(252)).round(4))
```

::: {.muted .small}
Ten years of Apple: about 1.8% on a typical day, 28% a year. Your Session 2 case
found 1.4% for 2024 alone, which was a calmer year.
:::


# Sorting

```{pyodide}
aapl.sort_values("ret").head(3)[["date", "close", "ret"]]
```

::: {.muted .small}
Apple's three worst days in ten years, dates and all.
:::


# Your turn [2]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Pick out Coca-Cola, then read off its daily volatility.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
ko = prices[prices["ticker"] == ...].copy()
ko["ret"] = ...
ko["ret"].std()
```




# Your turn 2: answer

```python
ko = prices[prices["ticker"] == "KO"].copy()
ko["ret"] = ko["close"].pct_change()
ko["ret"].std()
```

**About `0.0112`**, so 1.1% a day against Apple's 1.8%. The comparison you began
in Session 1 with two hand-picked prices, now over ten years.


# All eleven at once

Doing that eleven times would be eleven copies of the same code. Instead,
**group** by ticker and let pandas handle each group:

```{pyodide}
prices["ret"] = prices.groupby("ticker")["close"].pct_change()
prices.groupby("ticker")["ret"].std().sort_values(ascending=False)
```

::: {.muted .small}
The Session 2 loop, written once.
:::


# groupby, in words

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="960" height="215" viewBox="0 0 960 215" role="img" aria-label="groupby splits the table by ticker, applies a calculation to each group, and combines the answers">
  <rect x="30" y="55" width="150" height="105" rx="7" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="105" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" fill="#141413" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">one table</text>
  <text x="105" y="124" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">27,676 rows</text>
  <line x1="185" y1="107" x2="285" y2="107" stroke="#898781" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <polygon points="290,107 277,101 277,113" fill="#898781"/>
  <text x="236" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13.5" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" font-weight="600">split</text>
  <rect x="300" y="30" width="150" height="46" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <rect x="300" y="84" width="150" height="46" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <rect x="300" y="138" width="150" height="46" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="375" y="59" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14.5" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">AAPL</text>
  <text x="375" y="113" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14.5" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">KO</text>
  <text x="375" y="167" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14.5" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">... nine more</text>
  <line x1="455" y1="107" x2="555" y2="107" stroke="#898781" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <polygon points="560,107 547,101 547,113" fill="#898781"/>
  <text x="507" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13.5" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" font-weight="600">apply</text>
  <text x="507" y="132" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#898781" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">.std()</text>
  <rect x="575" y="30" width="120" height="46" rx="6" fill="#fbf4e4" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <rect x="575" y="84" width="120" height="46" rx="6" fill="#fbf4e4" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <rect x="575" y="138" width="120" height="46" rx="6" fill="#fbf4e4" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="635" y="59" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14.5" fill="#7a5a08" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">0.0179</text>
  <text x="635" y="113" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14.5" fill="#7a5a08" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">0.0112</text>
  <text x="635" y="167" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14.5" fill="#7a5a08" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">...</text>
  <line x1="700" y1="107" x2="790" y2="107" stroke="#898781" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <polygon points="795,107 782,101 782,113" fill="#898781"/>
  <text x="745" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13.5" fill="#0d366b" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" font-weight="600">combine</text>
  <rect x="805" y="55" width="130" height="105" rx="7" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="870" y="100" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" fill="#141413" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">one Series</text>
  <text x="870" y="124" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">11 answers</text>
</svg>
:::

Split, apply, combine. You will see this shape everywhere.


# Several summaries at once

```{pyodide}
prices.groupby("ticker")["ret"].agg(["mean", "std", "min", "max"]).round(4)
```

::: {.muted .small}
One row per stock. This is a risk report.
:::


# One column per stock

`pivot` reshapes the table: dates down the side, tickers across the top.

```{pyodide}
wide = prices.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close")
wide.tail(3)
```

::: {.muted .small}
The tall shape is better for storing and grouping. This wide one is better for
comparing stocks and for plotting, which is where we go next.
:::


# And it does maths column by column

```{pyodide}
wide.pct_change().std().sort_values(ascending=False).round(4)
```

Every column gets its own returns, then its own standard deviation. The whole
ranking, in one line.


# Picking out a date

Because the index is dates, you can slice it with dates:

```{pyodide}
wide.loc["2024-01-02":"2024-01-08", ["AAPL", "KO"]]
```

::: {.muted .small}
`.loc` works with the **labels** you can see. There is also `.iloc` for
positions, if you ever want "the last row" rather than a particular date.
:::


# 4 · matplotlib {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 4
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Drawing what you found, so somebody else can see it.
:::


# A figure is made of two things

::: {.diagram}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10.2, 3.0))
ax1 = fig.add_axes([0.085, 0.19, 0.375, 0.50])
ax2 = fig.add_axes([0.585, 0.19, 0.375, 0.50])
for ax, t, c in [(ax1, "AAPL", BLUE_LIGHT), (ax2, "KO", AMBER)]:
    s = WIDE.loc["2024", t]
    ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color=c, linewidth=1.5, label=t)
    ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
    ax.legend(loc="upper left")
    ax.tick_params(axis="x", labelsize=9.5)
    ax.set_xticks(ax.get_xticks()[::2])

fig.patches.append(Rectangle((0.012, 0.035), 0.976, 0.925,
                             transform=fig.transFigure, fill=False,
                             linestyle=(0, (6, 4)), edgecolor=AMBER,
                             linewidth=1.5, zorder=5))
fig.text(0.026, 0.895, "one Figure: the whole canvas", color="#7a5a08",
         fontsize=11.5, fontweight="600", va="center")
for x in (0.085, 0.585):
    fig.text(x, 0.775, "an Axes: one plot inside it", color="#7a5a08",
             fontsize=10.5, va="center")
plt.show()
```
:::

A **Figure** is the canvas; an **Axes** is one plot on it. Everything you draw,
you draw on an Axes.


# Every plot starts the same way

```{pyodide}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 2.4))
ax.plot([1, 2, 3, 4], [10, 12, 11, 14])
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
`plt.subplots()` hands you a figure and one axes. You draw on `ax`, and
`plt.show()` displays it.
:::


# Title, label, colour

```{pyodide}
aapl24 = wide.loc["2024", "AAPL"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.0))
ax.plot(aapl24.index, aapl24.values, color="#1c5cab", linewidth=1.6)
ax.set_title("Apple (AAPL) closing price, 2024", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Without the title and the label this is just a wiggly line. `color` takes any
colour name or hex code: change it and run the cell again.
:::


# Two lines, and a legend

```{pyodide}
p24 = wide.loc["2024"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.1))
ax.plot(p24.index, p24["AAPL"], label="AAPL")
ax.plot(p24.index, p24["KO"], label="KO")
ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
`label=` on each line, then one `ax.legend()`.
:::


# Your turn [3]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Give this plot a title and a y-axis label.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
nvda = wide.loc["2024", "NVDA"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.2))
ax.plot(nvda.index, nvda.values)
...
plt.show()
```


# Your turn 3: answer

```python
nvda = wide.loc["2024", "NVDA"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.2))
ax.plot(nvda.index, nvda.values)
ax.set_title("Nvidia (NVDA) closing price, 2024", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
plt.show()
```

A tripling over the year, and a reader who can tell what they are looking at.


# Four kinds of plot

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(13.2, 2.5))
r24 = RET.loc["2024"]

s = WIDE.loc["2024", "AAPL"]
axes[0].plot(s.index, s.values, color=BLUE_LIGHT, linewidth=1.4)
axes[0].set_title("ax.plot\nsomething over time", loc="left", fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_xticks([])

v = (r24.std() * np.sqrt(252)).sort_values()[-5:]
axes[1].barh(v.index, v.values, color=BLUE_LIGHT, height=0.62)
axes[1].set_title("ax.barh\ncomparing named things", loc="left", fontsize=12)
axes[1].grid(axis="y", visible=False)

axes[2].scatter(r24["SPY"], r24["AAPL"], s=9, color=BLUE_LIGHT, alpha=0.55,
                edgecolors="none")
axes[2].set_title("ax.scatter\none thing against another", loc="left",
                  fontsize=12)

axes[3].hist(r24["AAPL"], bins=30, color=BLUE_LIGHT)
axes[3].set_title("ax.hist\nthe shape of one variable", loc="left", fontsize=12)
axes[3].grid(axis="x", visible=False)

for a in axes:
    a.tick_params(labelsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Four calls, and the question decides which one you want. Everything else on a
plot is decoration you add afterwards.


# Bars, for comparing stocks

```{pyodide}
vol = wide.pct_change().std().sort_values()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.4))
ax.barh(vol.index, vol.values, color="#2a78d6")
ax.set_title("Daily volatility, 2015 to 2024", loc="left")
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
`barh` lays the bars down, which is the readable one when the labels are names.
`bar` stands them up.
:::


# A histogram, for a distribution

```{pyodide}
r = wide["AAPL"].pct_change().dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.2))
ax.hist(r, bins=50, color="#2a78d6")
ax.set_title("Apple daily returns, 2015 to 2024", loc="left")
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Most days are small moves near zero, with rare big ones out at the sides.
`bins` is how many bars to chop the range into.
:::


# The one-line version

pandas will plot itself, and fills in the axis and the legend for you:

```{pyodide}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.2))
wide.loc["2024", ["AAPL", "KO"]].plot(ax=ax)
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Good for a quick look while you work. Pass `ax=ax` and you can still add a title
afterwards, exactly as before.
:::


# The chart everybody makes first

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.0, 2.8))
sub = WIDE.loc["2019":]
for t in sub.columns:
    ax.plot(sub.index, sub[t], linewidth=1.1, label=t)
ax.set_title("Closing price, all eleven, 2019 onwards", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
ax.legend(ncol=6, loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```python
for t in wide.columns:
    ax.plot(wide.index, wide[t], label=t)
ax.legend()
```

Eleven lines, eleven colours, and a legend you have to keep looking back at.


# Say one thing instead

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.0, 2.8))
sub = WIDE.loc["2019":]
for t in sub.columns:
    if t != "NVDA":
        ax.plot(sub.index, sub[t], linewidth=1.0, color="#d8d7d0", zorder=1)
ax.plot(sub.index, sub["NVDA"], linewidth=2.0, color=BLUE, zorder=3)
ax.annotate("NVDA", xy=(sub.index[-1], sub["NVDA"].iloc[-1]),
            xytext=(-4, 9), textcoords="offset points",
            fontsize=12, fontweight="600", color=BLUE, ha="right")
ax.set_title("Nvidia against the rest of the universe, 2019 onwards",
             loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```python
for t in wide.columns:
    if t != "NVDA":
        ax.plot(wide.index, wide[t], color="#d8d7d0", linewidth=1.0)
ax.plot(wide.index, wide["NVDA"], color="#1c5cab", linewidth=2.0)
```

One line in colour, the rest in grey, and no legend to look back at.


# What to check before you show it

::: {.recap-grid}
::: {.recap-item}
**Does it have a title?**
Say what the reader is looking at.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Are the axes labelled?**
With units. "price (USD)", not "price".
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Can you tell the lines apart?**
A legend, or a name written on the line.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Do the bars start at zero?**
Lines need not. Bars must.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Is anything decorative?**
If it carries no information, remove it.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Would a stranger get it?**
Without you standing there to explain.
:::
:::


# It can look much better than this

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick
import numpy as np

s = WIDE["AAPL"]
dd = s / s.cummax() - 1
trough = dd.idxmin()
peak = s.loc[:trough].idxmax()

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11.0, 3.9))
gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 1, height_ratios=[3, 1], hspace=0.12,
                      left=0.085, right=0.965, top=0.78, bottom=0.11)
ax = fig.add_subplot(gs[0])
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1], sharex=ax)
for a in (ax, ax2):
    for side in ("top", "right"):
        a.spines[side].set_visible(False)
    a.grid(color="#ecebe4", linewidth=0.8)

ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color=BLUE, linewidth=1.5, zorder=3)
for i, alpha in enumerate(np.linspace(0.16, 0.01, 14)):
    ax.fill_between(s.index, s.values * (i / 14), s.values * ((i + 1) / 14),
                    color=BLUE, alpha=alpha, linewidth=0, zorder=1)
ax.set_ylim(0, s.max() * 1.08)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.StrMethodFormatter("${x:,.0f}"))
ax.tick_params(labelbottom=False)
ax.axvspan(peak, trough, color=AMBER, alpha=0.10, zorder=0)
ax.annotate("peak", xy=(peak, s.loc[peak]), xytext=(-52, 22),
            textcoords="offset points", fontsize=10, color="#7a5a08",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#c9a227", linewidth=1))
ax.annotate(f"worst drawdown  {dd.min():.0%}", xy=(trough, s.loc[trough]),
            xytext=(26, 72), textcoords="offset points", fontsize=10,
            color="#7a5a08",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#c9a227", linewidth=1))
ax.annotate(f"${s.iloc[-1]:,.0f}", xy=(s.index[-1], s.iloc[-1]), xytext=(6, -3),
            textcoords="offset points", fontsize=11, fontweight="600",
            color=BLUE)

ax2.fill_between(dd.index, dd.values, 0, color=AMBER, alpha=0.35, linewidth=0)
ax2.plot(dd.index, dd.values, color=AMBER, linewidth=0.9)
ax2.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter(1.0))
ax2.set_ylim(dd.min() * 1.15, 0.02)
ax2.set_ylabel("drawdown", fontsize=10)

axi = fig.add_axes([0.145, 0.495, 0.185, 0.19])
r = s.pct_change().dropna()
axi.hist(r, bins=60, color=BLUE, alpha=0.75)
for side in ("top", "right", "left"):
    axi.spines[side].set_visible(False)
axi.set_yticks([])
axi.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter(1.0, decimals=0))
axi.set_title("daily returns", fontsize=9, color="#52514e", loc="left", pad=3)
axi.grid(False)

fig.text(0.085, 0.93, "Apple, ten years", fontsize=17, fontweight="600",
         color=INK)
fig.text(0.085, 0.865,
         "Split-adjusted close, 2015 to 2024, with drawdown from the running peak",
         fontsize=10.5, color="#898781")
plt.show()
```


# And here is what that cost {.smaller}

```python
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 1, height_ratios=[3, 1], hspace=0.12)
ax, ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0]), fig.add_subplot(gs[1], sharex=ax)
for a in (ax, ax2):
    for side in ("top", "right"):
        a.spines[side].set_visible(False)
ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color="#1c5cab", linewidth=1.5, zorder=3)
for i, alpha in enumerate(np.linspace(0.16, 0.01, 14)):
    ax.fill_between(s.index, s.values * (i / 14), s.values * ((i + 1) / 14),
                    color="#1c5cab", alpha=alpha, linewidth=0, zorder=1)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.StrMethodFormatter("${x:,.0f}"))
ax.axvspan(peak, trough, color="#b8860b", alpha=0.10, zorder=0)
ax.annotate("peak", xy=(peak, s.loc[peak]), xytext=(-52, 22),
            textcoords="offset points", color="#7a5a08",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#c9a227"))
# ... and about thirty more lines for the drawdown panel, the inset
# histogram, the other annotations and the tick formatting
```

Every one of those lines is a small, ordinary call. There are just a lot of
them, and none of them is the analysis.


# So learn the four calls first

Get the plot right, then make it pretty, and only if it is worth the time.

::: {.keypoint}
When you do want it prettier, go to the **matplotlib gallery** at
`matplotlib.org`. It is a wall of example charts, each with the code that made
it. Find the picture closest to what you want, and read how it was done.
:::

::: {.muted .small}
That is how everybody learns plotting, including the people who do it for a
living.
:::


# Saving a figure

Now that you have a machine with folders on it:

```python
fig.savefig("figures/nvda_2024.png", dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
```

::: {.muted .small}
`dpi` is the resolution and `bbox_inches="tight"` trims the empty margin. Use
`.pdf` instead of `.png` for something that stays sharp at any size, which is
what you want in a thesis.
:::


# The whole ranking, in four lines

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

vol = (RET.loc["2024"].std() * np.sqrt(252)).sort_values()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.6, 2.8))
colors = [BLUE if t == "NVDA" else BLUE_LIGHT for t in vol.index]
ax.barh(vol.index, vol.values, color=colors, height=0.66)
for t, val in vol.items():
    ax.text(val + 0.006, t, f"{val:.0%}", va="center", fontsize=10.5,
            color="#52514e")
ax.set_xlim(0, vol.max() * 1.16)
ax.set_title("Annualised volatility, 2024", loc="left")
ax.grid(axis="y", visible=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```python
wide = prices.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close")
vol = (wide.loc["2024"].pct_change().std() * np.sqrt(252)).sort_values()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.barh(vol.index, vol.values)
```


# What stays with you {.smaller}

::: {.recap-grid}
::: {.recap-item}
**Your own machine**
Install once, `import` in every notebook.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Looking things up**
Hover, or `help`. It is on your machine, not online.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**NumPy**
Arrays do arithmetic all at once. `@` for matrices.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**pandas**
`read_csv`, filter, new columns, `groupby`, `pivot`.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**matplotlib**
`fig, ax = plt.subplots()`, then label everything.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Judgment**
The form, the scale and the baseline decide what a reader sees.
:::
:::


# The course case, Part 3 [case]{.chip .chip-case}

The risk report grows from two stocks to the whole universe.

::: {.keypoint}
Load all eleven with pandas, compute returns and volatility for every one of
them at once, and make the figures a reader could act on. Same investigation, no
loops.
:::


# Case Part 3: this week

`session_03_case.ipynb`, done at home before Session 4:

- load `prices.csv` yourself, and check it before trusting it
- daily returns and annualised volatility for all eleven, with `groupby`
- confirm your Session 2 answer for Apple, computed a different way
- rank the universe by risk, and plot the ranking
- look at how the stocks move together, and what that means for a portfolio

::: {.muted .small}
Hints and full solutions are folded into the notebook. Work first, then check.
:::


# Before Session 4

<div class="pillars">
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Homework</div><div class="pillar-title">Exercises</div><div class="pillar-body"><code>session_03_exercises.ipynb</code>: NumPy, pandas and matplotlib, plus practice at looking things up. Hints and solutions folded in.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Homework</div><div class="pillar-title">Case Part 3</div><div class="pillar-body"><code>session_03_case.ipynb</code>: the risk report, all eleven stocks, with figures.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Do it once</div><div class="pillar-title">Set up locally</div><div class="pillar-body">Python, VS Code and the three packages. The links are on the course page. Bring any error you get.</div></div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
**Next session:** what machine learning actually is. Observations, features and
targets, and why a model that fits your data perfectly is usually worthless.
:::
